== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:[[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[ja:[[Category:Shikinaisha shrines]] [[ja:[[Category:Ken-sha]] [[ja:[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[ja:[[Category:Kawachi Province]] [[ja:[[Category:Chihayaakasaka Village]] [[ja:[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tate Mikumari Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Tate Mikumari Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:[[ja:[[ja:建水分神社]]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Taka-mikura|ja|天水分神}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Kunisuibun-no-Kami|ja|国水分神}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Upper in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.

=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Upper in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Upper in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Upper Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Upper in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.

The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.

Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.

=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.

In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Uqqer in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Uqqer in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (Uqqer Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, Uqqer in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."

The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|12=draft}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|式内社|" to "|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|"
revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村|12=draft}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)|12=draft}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|12=draft}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|12=draft}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|12=draft}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|12=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|12=draft}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}, note the shrine's elevation to court ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|12=draft}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|12=draft}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft}}. The shrine received the highest rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|12=draft}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|12=draft}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村|12=draft|12=draft}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)|12=draft|12=draft}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|12=draft|12=draft}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft|12=draft}}, note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|12=draft|12=draft}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|12=draft|12=draft}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|12=draft|12=draft}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]
