[[水分神|国水分神]]
[[瀬織津姫|瀬織津姫神]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（小）、旧[[府社]] |創建 = [[崇神天皇]]5年 |本殿 = 水分造 |別名 = 上水分社 |例祭 = [[4月25日]]
10月第三[[土曜日]] |地図 = Japan Osaka |ラベル位置 = bottom }} '''建水分神社'''（たけみくまりじんじゃ）は、[[大阪府]][[南河内郡]][[千早赤阪村]]大字水分（すいぶん）にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]で、旧[[社格]]は[[府社]]。通称'''水分神社'''（すいぶんじんじゃ）。'''水分大明神'''、'''上水分社'''（うえのすいぶんのやしろ）とも称する。古より[[金剛山 (金剛山地)|金剛山]]の鎮守として、また、[[楠木氏]]の[[氏神]]として崇敬された。当社は[[錦織神社]]（[[富田林市]]）、[[美具久留御魂神社]]（下水分社、富田林市）とともに「[[河内国]]の三水分（みくまり）社」と呼ばれている。 == 祭神 == 中殿に[[天之御中主神|天御中主神]]、左殿に[[水分神|天水分神]]・[[ミヅハノメ|罔象女神]]、右殿に[[水分神|国水分神]]・[[瀬織津姫|瀬織津姫神]]を祀る。このうち天御中主神以外は[[水神]]である。 == 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[崇神天皇]]5年（[[紀元前1世紀|紀元前92年]]）に諸国が飢饉となった際、天皇は各地に溜池や溝を作ることを勧められた。この時に[[金剛山 (金剛山地)|金剛山]]の山麓に水分神が祀られたのに始まる。[[延長 (元号)|延長]]5年（[[927年]]）に編纂された[[延喜式神名帳]]に「[[河内国]][[石川郡 (大阪府)|石川郡]] 建水分神社」と記載されている。「上水分社」の呼称は、[[美具久留御魂神社]]（[[富田林市]]宮町）を下水分社と称するのに対応したものである。 また、『[[日本三代実録]]』には[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]5年（[[863年]]）に正五位下、貞観16年（[[874年]]）に従四位下、[[元慶]]3年（[[879年]]）に従四位上が朝廷より授けられたとある。 元の鎮座地は現在地より北約100mの水越川のほとりにあったが、当社を[[氏神]]としている地元の侍の[[楠木正成]]が[[鎌倉幕府]]に抵抗した[[後醍醐天皇]]側に付き、[[赤坂城の戦い]]、次いで[[千早城の戦い]]を近辺で繰り広げたために兵火に掛かって荒廃した。このため[[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）に後醍醐天皇の勅命を受けた楠木正成により、現在地に本殿、拝殿、鐘楼などを再建し遷座した。[[延元]]2年（[[1337年]]）[[4月27日 (旧暦)|4月27日]]、最高位である[[正一位]]の[[神階]]を授けられた。またこの年、楠木正成を祀る[[南木神社]]が摂社として境内に設けられている。 [[延元]]5年/[[暦応]]3年（[[1340年]]）に正成の嫡男[[楠木正行|正行]]が当社に奉納した扁額には「[[左衛門少尉]]」の自筆が記されている。 [[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]、[[織田信長]]による河内国侵攻の際には拝殿などが戦火を受けて焼失し、社領を没収されてしまい衰退するが、[[豊臣秀吉]]が社領を寄進し復興された。 [[1873年]]（[[明治]]6年）の[[近代社格制度]]制定の際に付近18ヵ村の総鎮守産土神の故を以て郷社に列格した。[[1907年]]（明治40年）に神饌幣帛料供進社に指定され、氏子地域内にある17の神社を[[神社合祀|合祀]]し、[[1913年]]（[[大正]]2年）に[[府社]]に昇格した。 戦前、楠木正成は天皇のために尽くした忠臣として大いに称えられたため、当社と摂社・南木神社には多くの参拝者が訪れた。 == 境内 == * 本殿 3棟 - 本殿は三殿で構成されている。中殿は一間社[[春日造]]で左右両殿は二間社[[流造]]、それを渡廊で連結させるという全国でも唯一の珍しい様式で「水分造」とも呼ばれている。構造の細部も優秀巧緻で建築上の模範とされる。[[1900年]]（[[明治]]33年）に[[大阪府]]の[[神社建築]]として初めて[[古社寺保存法]]に基づく特別保護建造物（現行法の「[[重要文化財]]」に相当）に指定され、[[1950年]]（[[昭和]]25年）、[[文化財保護法]]施行に伴い重要文化財となった。通常は非公開である。 ** 中殿（重要文化財） - [[建武 (日本)|建武]]元年（[[1334年]]）に[[後醍醐天皇]]の勅命で[[楠木正成]]により再建。 ** 左殿（重要文化財） - 建武元年（1334年）に後醍醐天皇の勅命で楠木正成により再建。 ** 右殿（重要文化財） - 建武元年（1334年）に後醍醐天皇の勅命で楠木正成により再建。 * 神門 * 幣殿 * 拝殿（割拝殿） * 大鳥居 - [[元禄]]3年（[[1690年]]）造立。笠木の長さ10.6m、高さ7.1mで近代以前の石造鳥居としては全国有数の規模とされる。掛けられている勅額「正一位 水分大明神」は後醍醐天皇の宸筆である。もともとは[[楠木正行]]が奉納した後醍醐天皇宸筆の勅額が掛けられていたが、木製のため額の表面が摩滅してしまったので[[宝永]]2年（[[1705年]]）に前[[大納言]]・[[葉室頼孝]]が磨滅した後醍醐天皇筆の文字をなぞって金銅製にて作り直したものが現在掛けられているものである。 * 狛犬 - [[万延]]元年（[[1860年]]）造立。石造の狛犬としては大阪府下で最大級である。 * 鐘楼 - [[神仏習合]]時代の名残である。[[太平洋戦争]]中に[[金属類回収令]]により寺院などから梵鐘が供出されていくなか、当社は楠木正成ゆかりの神社であったためか、梵鐘の供出は免れた。 * 祭器庫 * 旧宝物庫（国[[登録有形文化財]]） * 神輿庫 * 旧絵馬堂 * 社務所 * 境外 ** 御旅所 - 当社の北西約1kmの地にある。 *** 御旅所古墳 *** 御旅所北古墳 === 摂末社 === * 南木神社（なぎじんじゃ） - 祭神：楠木正成。摂社。詳細は「[[南木神社]]」を参照。 ** 本殿（国登録有形文化財） ** 幣殿（国登録有形文化財） ** 拝殿（国登録有形文化財） ** 築地塀（国登録有形文化財） * 金峯神社（きんぶじんじゃ） - 祭神：[[天照大御神]]。末社。 == 文化財 == === 重要文化財 === * 本殿 3棟 - 中殿、左殿、右殿からなる。 === 国登録有形文化財 === * 宝庫 * 南木神社本殿 * 南木神社拝殿及び幣殿 * 南木神社築地塀 === 千早赤阪村指定有形文化財 === * 建水分神社の延元5年扁額 === 千早赤阪村指定有形民俗文化財 === * 建水分神社の木造狛犬群 8体 == 例祭 == * 4月25日：春祭 - この日は楠木正成の誕生日とされていることから春祭は俗称「くすのきさん」と呼ばれている。 * 10月第三土曜日：秋祭 - 御旅所にて御神輿を中心に19台の[[地車]]が集まる風景は、河内随一といわれる。各地車には舞台が付いており（石川型だんじり）そこで上方芸能の原点ともいわれる「河内にわか」が奉納される。 == その他 == * [[大日本帝国海軍|日本海軍]]の[[戦艦]][[金剛 (戦艦)|金剛]]の[[艦内神社]]には[[1930年]]（昭和5年）より建水分神社の御分霊の神鏡が奉安されていた。 == 外部リンク == *[http://www.takemikumari.com/ 建水分神社オフィシャルホームページ] *[http://7kamado.net/shrine-index.html ななかまど-神社ふり～く]（合祀された神社に詳しい） *{{Commonscat-inline}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たけみくまりしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧府社]] [[Category:大阪府の重要文化財]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:修験道]] [[Category:楠木氏|社みなとかわしんしや]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の建築物]] [[Category:千早赤阪村の歴史]]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1913.

The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.

[[sn:Tate Mikumari Shrine]] [[ja:建水分神社]] [[en:Tate Mikumari Shrine]] [[sn:Take Mikumari Shrine]]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Chihayaakasaka Village|ja|千早赤阪村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chihayaakasaka Village|qq=}}, Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Mount Kongō|ja|金剛山 (金剛山地)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kongō|qq=}} and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Province|qq=}}."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=}}, while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qq=}}, note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|ja|楠木正成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masashige|qq=}} against the {{ill|Kamakura Shogunate|ja|鎌倉幕府|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura Shogunate|qq=}}. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|ja|後醍醐天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Go-Daigo|qq=}}. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Nobunaga|qq=}}'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=}}. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the {{ill|Japanese battleship Kongō|ja|金剛 (戦艦)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese battleship Kongō|qq=}} as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chihayaakasaka|Chihayaakasaka Village]], Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province
revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chihayaakasaka|Chihayaakasaka Village]], Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.text:
'''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chihayaakasaka|Chihayaakasaka Village]], Minamikawachi District, [[Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Takemikumari-jinja1.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Chihayaakasaka|qid=Q1072574}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.460333|N|135.632028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity=Central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] Left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} Right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}.}}{{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11486674]] Wikidata name: Takemimakuri Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takemikumari Shrine''' (建水分神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Chihayaakasaka|Chihayaakasaka Village]], Minamikawachi District, [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=|1=Kusunoki clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541208}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1913.

Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=|1=Kusunoki clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541208}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]

It is classified as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor shrine) and formerly held the Rank of a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Prefectural shrine]] under the old shrine Ranking system. Commonly referred to as "Suibun Shrine" (水分神社), it is also known as "Suibun Daimyōjin" and "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine). Historically, it has been venerated as the guardian shrine of [[:en:Mount Kongō|Mount Kongō]] and as the clan deity of the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|qq=|1=Kusunoki clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11541208}}. Along with {{ill|Nishikori Shrine|ja|錦織神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikori Shrine|qq=}} in Tondabayashi and {{ill|Mikukurumitama Shrine|ja|美具久留御魂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikukurumitama Shrine|qq=}} (Shimosuibun Shrine), it is one of the "Three Suibun Shrines of [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]]."
== Enshrined Deities == The shrine's central hall enshrines [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]], while the left hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Ame no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuhanome|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizuhanome|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}}, and the right hall enshrines {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|fr|lt=Kuni no Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikumari|qq=}} and {{ill|Sae-no-Kami|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sae-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}. All deities except Ame-no-Minakanushi are water gods.
== History == According to shrine tradition, the shrine was established in the 5th year of Emperor Sujin's reign (92 BCE) during a famine when the emperor encouraged the construction of reservoirs and canals. The shrine was built at the base of Mount Kongō to enshrine the water deity. The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.

The {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, compiled in 927, lists the shrine as "Kawachi Province, Ishikawa District, Takemikumari Shrine." The designation "Kami Suibunsha" (U11er Suibun Shrine) contrasts with "Shimosuibun Shrine" (Mikukurumitama Shrine) in Miyamachi, Tondabayashi.
Historical records, including the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], note the shrine's elevation to court Ranks: Senior Fifth Rank, Lower in 863; Junior Fourth Rank, Lower in 874; and Junior Fourth Rank, U11er in 879.
Originally located along the Mizukoshi River, the shrine was devastated during conflicts led by [[:en:Kusunoki Masashige|Kusunoki Masashige]] against the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura Shogunate]]. In 1334, Masashige relocated the shrine to its current site and rebuilt its structures, including the main hall, worship hall, and bell tower, by imperial decree from [[:en:Emperor Go-Daigo|Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The shrine received the highest Rank of Shoichii (Senior First Rank) on April 27, 1337. That same year, Masashige was deified in the shrine’s precincts as a subordinate deity in the {{ill|Nangi Shrine|ja|南木神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nangi Shrine|qq=}}.
In later centuries, the shrine faced further destruction during [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]'s invasion of Kawachi Province but was subsequently restored by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. In the modern era, the shrine was designated a {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prefectural shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} in 1913.
== Grounds == The shrine features several unique and culturally significant structures: * '''Main Hall''' (Important Cultural Property) – The three-part structure, known as "Suibun-zukuri," is unique in Japan. The central hall is built in the Kasuga-zukuri style, while the side halls are in the Nagare-zukuri style, linked by a passageway. * '''Great Torii Gate''' – Constructed in 1690 (Genroku 3), this stone torii is one of the largest pre-modern torii gates in Japan. The inscription "Shoichii Suibun Daimyōjin" is attributed to Emperor Go-Daigo. * '''Bell Tower''' – A remnant of the shrine's syncretic Shinto-Buddhist past. * '''Nangi Shrine''' (Subordinate Shrine) – Enshrines Kusunoki Masashige. Structures include the main hall, worship hall, and surrounding walls, all registered as tangible cultural properties.
== Festivals == * '''Spring Festival (April 25)''' – Also known colloquially as "Kusunoki-san," as it coincides with Kusunoki Masashige's birthday. * '''Autumn Festival (Third Saturday of October)''' – Features a grand procession of portable shrines and danjiri floats, showcasing the cultural heritage of Kawachi Province.
== Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Main Hall – Comprising the central, left, and right halls.
=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Treasure House * Structures of Nangi Shrine
== Other == A fragment of the shrine's spirit was enshrined aboard the [[:en:Japanese battleship Kongō|Japanese battleship Kongō]] as part of its shipboard shrine in 1930.
== External Links == * [http://www.takemikumari.com/ Official Website of Takemikumari Shrine]
